data.title: Kiniksa: Arcalyst Is The Engine, But KPL-387 Is The Accelerator
data.description:
Kiniksa (KNSA) is currently at an important inflection point where the focus has changed from commercial execution risk to expanding their pipeline of products and increasing their margins. Instead of being concerned with their ability to survive, Kiniksa can now focus on becoming the dominant player in the pericarditis space. The company has seen a significant increase in their cash reserves over the last twelve months, and they have a clear path to achieving blockbuster status with their lead product. I believe this has greatly reduced the risk profile for Kiniksa, and the company presents a solid opportunity based on its current financial state and outlook.

Recent Financial Updates

Kiniksa reported unaudited net product revenue of $677.5 million for 2025, up 62% when compared to 2024 ($417.0 million). More than just the result of the initial stage of the launch process, Kiniksa has demonstrated deeper penetration into the addressable market, as evidenced by the forecasted revenue range for 2026 ($900 million to $920 million) - if achieved, it would put Arcalyst within striking distance of the blockbuster milestone of $1 billion in annual sales.

As of the end of 2025, Kiniksa had $414.1 million in cash, cash equivalents, and short-term investments. Not only is this amount large, but it also represents an increase of $170.4 million in cash and equivalents during 2025, which means Kiniksa is generating significant amounts of free cash flow from operations. Unless there is a complete collapse in revenue or some unforeseen and previously unannounced, large-scale increase in operating expenses, Kiniksa does not need to worry about a cash runway to fund growth like most biotechs. Because they have essentially become self-funding, Kiniksa has removed one of the largest overhangs that tends to suppress valuation multiples for biotechs, which often have the threat of dilution looming over them.

That said, we need to carefully review the details of how Kiniksa reports net income because of their partnership with Regeneron (REGN). The collaboration agreement provides for a 50/50 profit sharing arrangement. Kiniksa records the top-line revenue, while after recording cost of goods sold, field force expenses, and commercial marketing expenses, Kiniksa splits the remaining operating profit 50/50 with Regeneron. During the third quarter of 2025, Kiniksa recorded $156.8 million in total operating expenses, including a very large $63.3 million line item for a collaboration expense paid to Regeneron. However, Kiniksa was able to post net income of $18.4 million for the quarter, which demonstrates that the model is working: the volume of sales has exceeded the substantial fixed and variable costs associated with the collaboration, resulting in net income being distributed to shareholders. As revenue grows towards the forecasted $920 million for 2026, the fixed portion of commercial expenses will decrease as a percent of revenue, and could allow for operating leverage in spite of the 50/50 gross profit split.

Pipeline Evolution

While Arcalyst is the current engine, the long-term value of Kiniksa will be based on the company's ability to evolve the franchise. The Company is now advancing KPL-387, a monoclonal antibody IL-1 receptor antagonist through a Phase 2/3 clinical trial as a treatment for recurrent pericarditis. At first glance, to some people it may appear unnecessary to develop another drug for the same disease area as their existing approved drug which has a strong market position. But when you take a closer look at the drug's mechanism of action and Kiniksa's business model, it becomes apparent that evolving the franchise with KPL-387 is a necessary step to realize maximum shareholder value.

The pharmacological mechanism of Arcalyst involves an IL-1 trap that is delivered via a weekly infusion requiring reconstitution. Although Arcalyst is effective, the inconvenience and burden of administration are likely to result in decreased adherence to treatment over time. A key benefit of KPL-387, which is a fully human monoclonal antibody, is that it could potentially be formulated as a liquid and administered subcutaneously on a monthly basis rather than weekly. The Phase 2 dose-ranging data, anticipated during the second half of 2026, will be a key catalyst for KPL-387. If KPL-387 can produce efficacy similar to Arcalyst but with once-monthly dosing, it will provide a compelling product upgrade for patients experiencing injection fatigue.

In addition to providing a product upgrade for patients, Kiniksa will also be able to retain 100% of the economics associated with each patient treated with KPL-387 (as opposed to Arcalyst). Presently, Kiniksa does not receive 100% of the profit associated with each patient treated with Arcalyst, because Regeneron receives a portion of it. Therefore, if the standard of care for recurrent pericarditis were to shift to KPL-387, Kiniksa would retain all of the economics associated with each patient, representing a substantial increase in gross margin. By developing KPL-387 internally, Kiniksa is creating a barrier to entry for generic competitors to Arcalyst, while creating the opportunity to double its economic capture per patient.

The Duration Mismatch: Commercial Insight

One of the key distinctions lost in most basic views of Kiniksa is the correlation between the length of time of therapy and the course of recurrent pericarditis. Pericarditis has typically been viewed as an acute, short-term inflammatory condition. However, Kiniksa's data supports a different perspective and provides a major commercial opportunity. Patients who have had multiple episodes of recurrent pericarditis have a median disease duration of almost 3 years. Historically, treatment for recurrent pericarditis has typically involved using tapered doses of either steroids or NSAIDs to manage symptoms of inflammation, but neither of these approaches addresses the underlying autoimmune-inflammatory process over the long-term.

The data currently available indicates that the average total duration of therapy with Arcalyst is growing and is now close to 3 years, which matches the median disease duration of patients. Synchronizing the length of therapy with the length of disease is highly sought after for treating chronic diseases. In essence, Arcalyst is no longer being used only to treat acute flare-ups of the disease, but instead, Arcalyst is being used to maintain the health of patients and modify their disease experience. This, in turn, will convert the revenue model from an acute episodic care model to a chronic care annuity model.

A major benefit of the patient stops metric seen in the patient flow data is the fact that while the number of active patients is increasing, patients do stop therapy. Reviewing the patient flow charts allows us to deduce that a substantial amount of these stops are likely due to the burden of receiving weekly injections, since one-third of patients continue to be symptomatic at five years. Where the benefits of reviewing the commercial data and the pipeline synergistically relate to each other is in the potential that the lost patients, those who stopped taking Arcalyst due to administration fatigue, but who still have the disease, represent a waiting reservoir of demand for KPL-387. Therefore, I believe KPL-387 will not only cannibalize Arcalyst, but also recapture the high churn segment of the market that Arcalyst cannot retain. The recapture rate of KPL-387 could significantly increase the total addressable market value by increasing the average LTV of a customer across the entire franchise.

Counterarguments and Risks

Although the company has a solid balance sheet, all of the revenue projections are dependent upon a single product (Arcalyst). Therefore, any issues that may occur in the production of Arcalyst, such as a manufacturing issue, a safety concern, or an adverse ruling by regulators, will have a negative effect on the valuation of the company.

In addition to the risk that exists due to the dependence upon a single product, Kiniksa's reliance upon the Regeneron partnership creates additional risks. Although the partnership provided the asset that allowed the company to be formed, the partnership also provides an upper limit to the potential earnings generated by the company from the sale of Arcalyst. Under the terms of the partnership, Kiniksa bears the expense of marketing and distributing the product and working with payers, but shares the earnings from the product equally with Regeneron. If Arcalyst generates lower-than-expected revenues, Kiniksa may bear the expense of maintaining a large-scale commercial organization, while sharing the profits with Regeneron.

In addition to the risks associated with the development and launch of Arcalyst, Kiniksa faces risks related to the development and launch of KPL-387. The mechanism of action for KPL-387 (inhibition of IL-1) is established through the success of Arcalyst (a fusion protein), however, there is no guarantee that the success achieved with a fusion protein can be replicated when using a monoclonal antibody. If Kiniksa is unable to achieve comparable results with KPL-387 in the Phase 2/3 trials, the thesis for expanding margins over time will collapse. Kiniksa will remain reliant upon the economics of the partnership developed with Arcalyst, which will restrict its ability to compare valuations to other companies with a portfolio of wholly-owned products.

Total Addressable Market and Competitive Landscape

The main target market includes the 14,000 U.S. patients that experience multiple recurrences of pericarditis annually. Penetration into the target market of these patients is only about 18% as of late 2025, thus providing substantial room for growth in terms of the number of patients that can be targeted. However, the largest area of potential expansion in the TAM for Kiniksa's product is the "first recurrence" patient population, which totals about 26,000 patients annually. If Kiniksa gains a foothold with this patient group, then it will effectively triple its potential patient base.

Kiniksa's data shows that about 20% of its Arcalyst patients are using the product on a regular basis when they first experience a recurrence of their disease. Early-line adoption of the product is important. If clinical practice continues to evolve as has been indicated by the American College of Cardiology's Concise Clinical Guidance document, which recommends IL-1 inhibition as a second-line treatment option for acute pericarditis, then the TAM for Kiniksa's product expands from a small refractory market to a much larger second-line standard-of-care market.

Kiniksa also enjoys a competitive moat. Currently, there are no other FDA-approved products to treat recurrent pericarditis. Therefore, the primary competition is through the off-label use of other drugs. Anakinra is another IL-1 receptor antagonist, however, it must be administered daily via injections, and many patients develop severe injection-site reactions; therefore, anakinra would be less favorable than either Arcalyst's weekly dosing regimen or the potential KPL-387 monthly dosing regimen for long-term maintenance. Other anti-inflammatory biologics that target IL-6 or TNF-alpha have not demonstrated the same level of specificity or efficacy in treating pericarditis as the IL-1 inhibitors.

Future Outlook

Kiniksa looks like it could have a year of execution and solid pipeline progress through 2026. The revenue guidance provided by the company indicates that it believes that the adoption curve has not slowed down. The most significant catalyst to watch will be the KPL-387 Phase 2 data release in the second half of the year. The positive data would effectively eliminate many of the risks associated with the long term prospects of the company, and allow for the company to move to a higher margin business model. Risks associated with the company include the concentration of revenue as well as the terms of partnership agreements. However, I believe the strength of the balance sheet, combined with the direction of revenue growth, suggests that the current valuation does not accurately represent the company's strategic position.

data.title: Q3 GDP Updated Estimate: Real GDP At 4.4%, Higher Than Expected
data.description:
Originally published on January 22, 2026

By Jennifer Nash

The U.S. economy showed robust growth in the third quarter, posting its strongest reading in two years. The BEA's updated estimate of real gross domestic product showed economic growth increased at an annual rate of 4.4% in Q3 2025. The latest estimate was higher than the 4.3% forecast and marked an uptick from the Q2 final estimate of 3.8%.

Here is the opening text from the Bureau of Economic Analysis news release:

Real gross domestic product (GDP) increased at an annual rate of 4.4 percent in the third quarter of 2025 (July, August, and September), according to the updated estimate released by the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis. In the second quarter, real GDP increased 3.8 percent. The increase in real GDP in the third quarter reflected increases in consumer spending, exports, government spending, and investment. Imports, which are a subtraction in the calculation of GDP, decreased.

Gross Domestic Product (GDP)

Real gross domestic product (GDP) measures how fast or slowly the economy is growing and measures the inflation-adjusted value of all goods and services produced by the economy. It is considered the broadest measure of economic activity and the primary indicator of an economy's health.

The Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA) releases real GDP data on a monthly basis. There are three versions released a month apart, advance, second, and final, each incorporating data that was previously unavailable. Economists can use GDP to determine whether an economy is growing or experiencing a recession.

This chart illustrates the annualized, inflation-adjusted change in U.S. gross domestic product (GDP) from the preceding quarter, with data beginning in Q2 1947. (Prior to Q2 1947, GDP was calculated annually.) For context, the chart also highlights recessions (as determined by the National Bureau of Economic Research, NBER), the historical GDP growth average of 3.20%, and the current 10-year moving average of 2.72%.

A key trend is evident: the 10-year moving average of GDP growth fell below the historical average in 2007 and has remained there, signaling a significant deceleration in U.S. economic expansion since the Great Recession. Current growth exceeds both the long-term series average and the 10-year moving average, indicating a faster pace of growth than experienced historically.

The chart above has many interesting data points but can be quite overwhelming to look at. This next chart provides a simplified look over the past few years.

Here is a log-scale chart of real GDP with an exponential regression, which helps us understand growth cycles since the 1947 inception of quarterly GDP. The regression illustrates the fact that the current trend, since the Great Recession, has a visibly lower slope than the long-term trend. In fact, the current GDP is 12.3% below the pre-recession trend (2008).

A particularly telling representation of slowing growth in the US economy is the year-over-year rate of change. Beginning in 1947, the average year-over-year rate at the start of recessions is 3.2%, with a range of 1.28% to 6.78%. The current year-over-year rate for real GDP is at 2.34%. The current level is at or below 7 of the 12 recession starts during this time frame.

GDP: A Look Ahead

In the chart below, we use the Conference Board's Economic Forecast for the U.S. Economy to visualize GDP forecast over the next few years.

January 14, 2026

Policy Crosswinds Raise Uncertainties around Growth Forecast

The US economy entered 2026 amid heightened uncertainty around evolving policy decisions.

While The Conference Board maintains expectations of slower growth through late 2025 and early 2026 because of tariffs, there are sizable risks both to the upside and downside. Nevertheless, the projections for both years were upgraded following the publication of an outsized real GDP gain in Q3 2025 and a sharp narrowing in the trade deficit at the start of Q4 2025. Inventories likely experienced a sizable drawdown in H2 2025. However, missing data, due to the federal government shutdown, obscures real economic activity. This in turn complicates forecasting the evolution of the economy in 2026.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Supply Chain 'Bottlenecks' May Be An Investment Opportunity
data.description:
Supply chain disruptions aren’t just a risk, they can be an investing opportunity. Benjamin Gossack, Managing Director and Co-Head of Global Equity Portfolio Management with TD Asset Management, joins MoneyTalk to discuss how “bottlenecks” may create potential openings for investors.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: We have seen some volatile markets in recent sessions, lots of geopolitical developments out there. But our guest today says, if you look past the noise, there are some positive signals for the markets. Joining us now to discuss is Ben Gossack, managing director and co-head of Global Equity Portfolio Management with TD Asset Management. Ben, welcome back to the program.

Benjamin Gossack: Thanks for having me back on the show. Excited to chat with you, Greg.

Greg Bonnell: Lots to chat about as well. It's a holiday-shortened week for the Americans for trading. Of course, they went into a long weekend. Some people were saying that Iran was top of mind heading to the weekend. By the time we came out of it, it was Greenland. In the end, a lot of headlines, a lot of noise. You're saying look past it. What should we be looking to?

Benjamin Gossack: Yeah. I mean, the way that we started 2026 is almost like we started 2025 with so many different policy changes. There's a ton of domestic policy changes in terms of capping interest rates and then, obviously, international forays. But there actually is still a lot of positivity going on.

And when I look around, markets are at new highs around the world, so it could be Canada, US, Japan, Europe. But what I've noticed is that a lot of the same trends that have been driving these indices continue to persist. So I know a lot of people think we rotate around the sun. There must be something new. It's a lot of the same same that I'm seeing, Greg.

Greg Bonnell: All the same, sames. Let's talk about some of the things we've been seeing in the markets because for a while, everyone was focused on the Magnificent Seven. They haven't been so magnificent. And yet we have markets closing at new highs without them.

Benjamin Gossack: Yeah. So I understand that the Mag Seven take up a big weight of the indices. And if you're just owning a portfolio of S&P 500, or TSX 60, or MSCI, I understand why you'd be very focused on these particular stocks. But being an active manager, we're looking for lots of opportunities, again, based on secular themes and trends.

And this has been a very broad market and much broader than people give it credit for. And that diversification has been going on, I'd say, for the past two years. And only now people are talking about because they see the Russell 2000 move, it must mean that other stocks are moving.

I will say this, Greg. Microsoft (MSFT) has been underperforming. Apple (AAPL) has been underperforming. Depending on the week, there may be a positive or negative catalyst for Tesla (TSLA). It's hard to stay on top of that. Broadcom (AVGO) has been lagging. I'd say -- and Meta (META) has been lagging. When I look at the Mag Seven, I would just say Google, the one that everyone had written off, has actually marched forward and looks to be the leader of that seven.

Greg Bonnell: OK. Let's talk about what the markets are rewarding these days. You say it is bottlenecks. Walk me through that thinking.

Benjamin Gossack: Yeah. So as we're going through various stocks, what we've been picking up is the market. So I'll say there's two things that I know about markets. They reward growth, and especially if that growth is led by pricing power, because if you're a company and you can raise prices and you keep your expenses the same, it flows right to the bottom line. So we like that type of story.

And asset prices tend to trend. They can trend up. They can trend down. They can trend sideways. They can go sinusoidal. But they do trend. And so what we're looking at here is, obviously, AI, the data center, power, these are very key themes that are driving markets. But specifically, what the market has been rewarding -- and like I said, they're not rewarding Broadcom right now. NVIDIA (NVDA) has been going sideways. They've been focused on the areas in the data center where there's just not enough supply.

And this is why if you've seen companies like a Micron (MU), or Western Digital (WDC), or Seagate (STX), or the companies that make the machines that make the memory, have all been going vertical, it's because, effectively, they've sold out of 2026 and now we're talking about how much might be sold out in 2027. And it's because the roadmap for these NVIDIA chips and these GPUs that power the data center, every new design requires even more memory. And there's just been a shortage of memory. And that means that these companies have pricing power.

Greg Bonnell: That sounds like a good news story for those companies that produce the stuff. Not such a good news story for the end user of those components.

Benjamin Gossack: Yeah. And this is, again, I'm always fascinated by markets and how humans -- if it's great for them, it must be bad for someone else. And the market has really figured this out. So everything that we use in our lives has got circuits, and memory, and CPUs. And so you can imagine if everything is being dedicated now for memory for the data center, well, then it must be impacting other areas. And one of the areas that we are seeing is consumer electronics.

And so we haven't seen the company's report yet. But the market doesn't want to wait for that. They've already started to anticipate that companies like an Apple, like a Nintendo (NTDOY), like a Sony (SONY) -- so major consumer electronic companies are going to have difficulty in terms of handling those inflationary forces that they're seeing in their cost base from these memory devices. So maybe a company like Apple might be able to try to manage that. But if we look at a company like Nintendo, already the market's anticipating that this is going to be a problem in terms of their margins.

Greg Bonnell: Interesting stuff there. Now let's talk about banks and insurance firms. How are they looking?

Benjamin Gossack: So banks in general have been looking great. Banks in Canada, banks in the US, specifically money center banks and investment banks, and then banks in Europe -- I've tried to pound the table with you many, many times on this show was not a 2025 story but has been a story for now the past five years. Even banks in Japan are breaking out to new highs.

In particular, I'm very focused on Canadian banks. I know we've been flashing the charts. We haven't really focused on it, so maybe we'll spend a little bit of time on this chart. This is a line that represents a tug of war, Greg, between Canadian banks and the TSX60. And I've tried to really highlight that with an arrow pointing up and an arrow pointing down.

So if you see this curve pointing up, it means the Canadian banks have relative strength. And if we start to see that go down, the Canadian banks, you may say, hey, they're up 2%. They're up 3%. But if the TSX60 is up 10 because gold's rallying, well, then as an active manager, you're underperforming by owning these banks. So that's why this is really important.

This particular chart was very helpful last year around this time because this is when Trump initially came after Canada and Mexico in terms of trade, and tariff, and all that type of stuff before Liberation Day. And we got a glimpse of what could be our future in terms of what we saw within, let's say, the last 48 hours, in terms of thoughts about Greenland, and the Arctic, and trade, and all that type of stuff. And so you can see the Canadian banks start to pull back a bit.

Now no chart goes up all the way so stocks do tend to move forward, pull back. But you're watching that and you're watching the same sort of stuff going on. And you start to think, could there be-- is this the market starting to think about USMCA, or CUSMA, and where they think Canada sits in the negotiation table?

We have seen some bit of a relief so it's crazy yesterday. We think we're going -- Trump's going really hard on Greenland. And then within a few hours, we have a deal. There was a relief rally in the Canadian banks.

So I would say, since last year, the people that invest in Canadian banks have looked past USMCA, CUSMA. I do think that started to creep up into their attention. Where do we stand? What could be the resolution? But a deal wants to be made. And that typically has been the game plan from this presidency and this administration. So I think that's where the stocks have been going.

Greg Bonnell: So there's the Canadian banks. How about insurance firms? And how about the credit situation?

Benjamin Gossack: Yeah. So insurance is a theme I really like. It's a service that everyone needs. Every well-functioning economy needs insurance. We had this great cycle. They call it a hard market where companies are able to raise their premiums. But now we've shifted into a soft cycle. And this is when the stocks tend to underperform. What people are looking at is a basket of US insurance companies relative to the S&P 500. And so I'd say, kind of late first quarter, early second quarter of last year, just pushing to new highs. And they've been unwinding themselves.

So there's just the natural order of things. There's the cycle. And you have to understand you want to be overweight when we have a hard market. You want to pull back capital when you have a soft market. But what I want to highlight -- and I think this is going to be a theme that persists all year -- we are talking about affordability in the US, particularly around utility bills. But I also think about insurance premiums. And insurance premiums, when it comes to the individual, whether you're insuring your house or your car as opposed to a business insuring their business, is controlled by the government.

And we have 36, so 3-6, governor races in the US this year. And so this is going to be a key top of mind. Why is my utility bill going up because there's data centers that are coming into my neighborhood? And what are you doing about these insurance premiums because it's getting ridiculous to insure my house and car?

Greg Bonnell: Insurance. Let's round out the conversation with credit you say is looking better.

Benjamin Gossack: Yeah. So people can turn on the TV. They can read the newspaper. They can get emotional about what they're seeing. But one thing that is important about markets is credit. This is a society and economy built on credit. This is comparing high-yield corporates. So this would be the riskier area of the bond market relative to US Treasurys. And I've tried to match the durations here and US Treasurys, let's assume, being the safest form of paper.

As we entered the year, we were sort of rolling a bit, which was giving me some pause, but we weren't broken. But now this is reaccelerating in favor of the high-yield corporate. So if they're pulling on the tug of war, that little flag starting to move towards them, that just tells me that investors are feeling more confident about credit. And if they're feeling more confident about credit, I think that's good in general. And it does match up well with all the other measures in terms of risk on, risk off. So you may see certain stocks up and down. But in general, I'd say the environment is quite healthy.

Original Post

data.title: Groupe Dynamite: When The Bottom Never Comes
data.description:
Last October, I told you I was itching for a pullback in Groupe Dynamite (GRGD:CA) - a fast-growing Canadian apparel retailer—so I could finally snag a piece of this gem.

With the valuation stretched and insider buying on pause, I figured the market would reprice Groupe Dynamite to somewhere around $40–$50, just enough for us to take a bite.

Even then, I flagged plenty of room for value creation. Sitting on more than $250 million in cash and a squeaky-clean balance sheet, Dynamite could either return capital via dividends and buybacks or go on the hunt for M&A.

After I tagged it as a ‘Hold’ with an outperform tilt, Dynamite tore past the S&P, racking up just over 25% total return while the S&P limped along at 2% and the TSX managed 9%.

Honestly, I didn’t expect the momentum to be this strong.

But it wasn't luck.

On top of raising its FY 2025 guidance across the board, Dynamite dropped a special dividend of $2.30 per share, which works out to a ~3% yield at today’s prices. Insiders were snapping up shares hard in both December and January.

So, I want to take a deeper look at the year-end results and recheck the valuation, now that multiples have pulled back after earnings expectations.

Well-Dressed Results

In my last write-up, I pointed out that even with consumer confidence in the gutter, Canadian shoppers were still opening their wallets for discretionary spending.

Broadly speaking, real wages are still creeping higher at a low single-digit pace (~1.1% in Q3, up from Q2), and most of that growth is coming out of Ontario. When it comes to clothing, spending was up around 11.6% in FY 2025, and Dynamite chipped in about 3.2% of that growth in Q3 alone (September to November).

That’s a pretty eye-catching sequential improvement.

If you take a closer look, the Discretionary Goods category is slowly catching up to Discretionary Services and, even more so, to Essentials.

In Quebec, which is the company’s largest footprint, expenses were up just 0.6% in the quarter, slowing from Q2, and it looks like Dynamite clearly outperformed there.

December did bring a few bumps, even though it sits outside Dynamite’s Q3 FY 2025. Food inflation moved up from 4.2% in November to 6.2% year-over-year, the highest level since August 2023.

Entertainment spending pulled back 0.7% month-over-month, while apparel demand has, so far, stayed relatively firm.

Despite a few yellow flags in the background, Dynamite put up a very strong 31.6% gain in same-store sales for the quarter, up from 28.6% in Q2. Revenue rose to $363 million, a 40.7% increase.

The momentum didn’t fade after that. Sales held up well through Christmas and the year-end holidays, and early into Q4 FY 2025 (December through January 3), same-store sales were still running at a healthy 30.8%.

Even without a neat traffic and pricing split, you can tell a good part of the upside is coming from higher volumes. But don’t forget they’re also leaning into a more luxury-inspired approach.

Our values-led culture continues to drive disciplined execution, resulting in strong holiday performance. This momentum, supported by our selective real estate strategy and rising digital engagement, is driving meaningful margin expansion and reflects the strength of our luxury-inspired operating model.

And here, we can look at this from a three layers:

  1. As you might expect from a luxury-inspired brand, Dynamite is increasingly selling clothes close to full price, with the markdown structurally decreasing as the positioning works.
  2. Lower freight costs (explicitly stated by the CEO in the press release).
  3. Dilution of SG&A fixed costs (from 30.9% of sales in Q3 FY 2024 to 26.4% in the last quarter).

It also shows up in the EBITDA margin, climbing right along with the top line:

With a choppier, tariff-driven consumer backdrop, everyone seems focused on defending margins, just using different playbooks.

Zumiez (ZUMZ), for example, is pushing more private labels into the mix, while LuxExperience (LUXE) keeps nudging NET-A-PORTER further upmarket.

If you stack these side by side, Dynamite looks like it’s doing the better job.

One of the big reasons I've liked this stock all along is how much FCF it consistently generates.

That gives them plenty of room to open new stores, hand cash back to shareholders, and opportunistically scoop up distressed brands.

In this quarter they generated $146.4 million in OCF, which, after deducting the $24.7 million in Capex, gives us a hefty FCF of $121.7 million.

Of course, if you discount the positive change in operating assets, the adjusted FCF would be $81.1 million, still quite strong.

Starting to Distribute: Optionality Above All

Sticking with the cash flow angle, even without paying regular dividends or buying back stock, it's starting to feel like Dynamite can't just sit on all that cash forever.

With that in mind, management announced a special dividend of $2.30 per share this quarter.

That’s hardly going to put a dent in the balance sheet. With 108.3 million outstanding shares, the payout comes to roughly $249 million. Even assuming my FCF estimate of $180 to $201 million (likely toward the upper end), they would still be sitting on about $205.8 million in cash after topping it up from the balance sheet.

It feels like a way to keep their options open without locking themselves into a recurring quarterly payout.

Nothing material on the M&A front by the second half of FY 2026, and this starts to look like the kind of move they’d happily repeat, again at a low single-digit yield.

Tweaking My Price Target

At this point, it would almost be silly not to revisit the valuation, especially after the company bumped up its FY 2025 guidance.

Here’s what actually changed and what didn’t:

Pretty much all the changes were positive.

Same-store sales got a 650 bp bump in the outlook, the adjusted EBITDA margin range widened, and Capex actually came down. The only thing that didn’t budge was the target of opening 8 to 9 net new stores.

It feels like even they caught themselves by surprise.

Since we're looking at unadjusted EBITDA, I'll stick with a ~30–35% margin for FY 2026 as my base case, and I'll also run bull (36–38%) and bear (25–29%) scenarios. Anchoring on Wall Street’s consensus for next year, I get base case EBITDA in the $387 to $451.5 million range, $464.4 to $490.2 million in the bull scenario, and $322.5 to $374.1 million in the bear case.

On multiples, in the base case where Dynamite keeps growing and holds strong margins, I'm comfortable using 17 to 19x EBITDA, roughly in line with peers like Aritzia (ATZ:CA). In the bull case, that moves up to 20 to 22x, while the bear case compresses to 13 to 15x.

Using the midpoint EBITDA for each scenario and discounting it back to a roughly 9% cost of equity, we arrive at the following price targets:

  • Base: $57.30 to $63.85
  • Bull: $76.10 to $83.50
  • Bear: $37.10 to $42.50

Given that Dynamite is starting to look like a true compounder within apparel retail, I’m comfortable anchoring closer to the midpoint of the bull case, which puts fair value somewhere around $80 per share.

What’s interesting is that by comparing Dynamite to Aritzia and aiming for a P/E of about 39x, you end up very close to my bull case target of $87.

You could make the case that Aritzia deserves higher multiples, but Dynamite’s high-margin strategy could keep double-digit growth going for a while, perhaps even more consistently than Aritzia, which runs lower margins.

Let’s see what the other models have to say.

If you plug Graham’s Model with an EPS growth rate of about 16%, which is my CAGR through FY 2029 based on current Street estimates, you get to a price target slightly above $75, even after baking in a 25% margin of safety.

That doesn't change much even if you use $2.24 (the most optimistic FY 2026 EPS estimate), with the price target still landing around $77 per share.

Just to stress the sensitivity a little, if EPS CAGR ends up matching the FY 2024 to FY 2025 growth rate of about 20%, you're already looking at something closer to $93 per share, even after applying a margin of safety (upside of 21.8% from where we are).

Put simply, the stock seems to be discounting today’s estimates, but not a few more upside EPS surprises. And considering the company has beaten Street estimates in all five reported quarters so far, there isn't much room for doubt on that front.

So, all things considered, I’d call Dynamite a 'Buy'.

I don’t think we’ll see the kind of bottom that lets us pick it up at a discount. The company will probably keep beating expectations, and Wall Street will likely adjust estimates again.

I’m taking a slightly more conservative stance, and I’d peg $85 as a reasonable short-to-medium-term target.

data.title: Axon: Higher Compensation And Tariffs Lead To Warning
data.description:
Investment thesis

Axon Enterprise, Inc. (AXON) has been a high-flying growth stock, one that has rewarded shareholders with above-average capital gains. But the price has fallen significantly in recent months, paralleling a decline in net income and earnings.

As a result, a red banner across Axon’s summary page at Seeking Alpha warns that the company “is at high risk of performing badly,” and the system gives it a Sell rating.

In my analysis, I find the Seeking Alpha concerns are justified and that the firm is overpriced. However, because of that previous outperformance and the possibility of better results in the future, I rate it a Hold.

About Axon

The company provides technology solutions to law enforcement in the U.S. and internationally. It does that through two segments: Software & Services, and Connected Devices (formerly TASER).

The latter refers to the non-lethal weapons (a class of weapons known as CEDs or Conducted Energy Devices), which it develops, manufactures, and sells. It also provides accessories. TASER also was the company name until 2017.

Software & Services develops, manufactures, and sells integrated hardware and software solutions that assist in the collection and management of evidence.

Demand in both segments has been growing rapidly. In the Q3-2025 earnings report, Axon reported Software & Services revenue grew 41% year-over year, while Connected Devices revenue increased by 24% y-o-y.

But, while revenue has been growing quickly, net income and EPS have been dragging.

Latest earnings

The Q3 report came out after the close on November 4, 2025, and beat revenue estimates of $719.64 million by $5.81 million—but its non-GAAP EPS of $1.17 missed analyst estimates by $0.37.

The Seeking Alpha system provides this abbreviated income statement:

And this abbreviated balance sheet:

That non-GAAP earnings miss prompted many shareholders to sell. At the close on November 4, Axon stock traded at $706.13, and it dropped for 12 consecutive trading days, to $519.61, before regaining some of that lost ground.

A few days later, on November 13, the Quant system downgraded its rating from Hold to Strong Sell. That drop was driven by the valuation grade of F. As of January 19, it had improved its grade to D-, but still gave Fs for most ratios:

Price/Sales [TTM and FWD] received F grades, as did the Price/Book ratios.

The warning

Click on the red banner on Axon’s summary page, and you will see this explanation for the warning:

“Axon Enterprise, Inc. has characteristics which have been historically associated with poor future stock performance. AXON is overpriced and has decelerating momentum when compared to other Industrials stocks, to the point that it gets a Sell rating from our Quant rating system.”

In short, the Quant system finds Axon stock is too expensive and has been losing momentum. Let’s examine each of them in turn.

The Axon share price

First, this is a five-year chart showing the volatility of Axon’s price:

Back in mid-2022, shares were trading for less than $100.00, but by the summer of 2025 they had risen to almost $900.00 ($885.92 on August 5, the day after Q2 earnings were announced).

Investors did not like what they saw in that earnings report, and the price began to fall. Nor did they like the Q3 results, as noted above, and the price fell even further. So, the share price fell significantly, yet it still remained overvalued.

If the price has fallen as much as it has and the stock remains overvalued, then we need to look for answers in the earnings, since P/E is literally price divided by earnings. The following chart shows revenue, net income, and EPS over the past five years:

Looking at the price chart and the revenue-net-income-EPS chart shows that earnings were falling as the price was falling, so overvaluation continued.

Why are earnings falling?

In the Q1 earnings report, Axon attributed its operating loss of $9 million mainly to higher stock-based compensation of $140 million, broken out as follows:

  • COGS: $13 million
  • SG&A: $71 million
  • R&D: $56 million.

However, Axon also reported non-GAAP net income of $81 million.

The Q2 earnings report attributed its $1 million operating loss mainly to $139 million in stock-based compensation.

For Q3, the company pointed to $146 million in compensation, as well as an unspecified amount due to its increased headcount.

To put the compensation issue in greater context, it totaled $101.8 million in Q3-2024 and $146.2 million in Q3-2025. That’s an increase of 43.6% in one year. Expect a similar increase from full-year 2024 to full-year 2025.

Tariffs also contributed to lower earnings. On the Q3 earnings call, CFO Brittany Bagley told analysts, “Adjusted gross margin of 62.7% decreased 50 basis points year-over-year, primarily due to tariffs impacting us for the first full quarter.”

It was much the same story in the Q1 and Q2 earnings report, with the firm referring to U.S. and international tariffs in reporting Adjusted EBITDA results and guidance.

Given these barriers to profitability, especially when it comes to compensation, it is not surprising many shareholders have exited and moved their capital elsewhere. Quarterly reports prominently display revenue growth, but have nothing to say about higher compensation costs.

Basic GAAP EPS has fallen from $1.77 in Q4-2024 to a loss of $0.03 in Q3-2025, and there is no mention in company reports of turning around bottom-line results.

Considering these results and the outlook, I agree with the Quant system that Axon is overpriced.

Slowing momentum

While Axon has ample revenue momentum, it does not have net income or earnings momentum, as discussed above. Investors usually buy on the latter rather than revenue, so the “decelerating momentum” is a fair call.

These are the data on which the Quant call is based:

Historical price performance data shows that ownership previously had been far more rewarding than owning the S&P 500:

Note Axon’s total return versus that of the S&P 500. This is a company that did deliver big returns to its shareholders, raising the possibility it might do so again.

In this slide from the Q3-2025 earnings presentation, the company states its penetration of the total domestic law enforcement market is less than 15%:

There is no lack of domestic market upside, and indeed, Axon has quickly grown its share, judging by 30% year over year revenue increases. No doubt there are also many opportunities in the enterprise sector and internationally.

The challenge is to keep its costs down. On another slide, it writes, “Strong, Consistent Growth with Attractive Gross Margin Profile”. That is promising, but it does not cover the SG&A section of the income statement. As we’ve seen, compensation costs through SG&A and R&D are heavy.

There are no references from management about how this situation might be reversed or how it might increase productivity in other areas.

I agree with the Quant system that momentum is slowing. And since its shares also are overpriced and there is no apparent plan for recovery, it is hard to recommend it to investors.

Target prices and ratings

The overvaluation point is made in the valuation table, where its P/E ratios and one PEG ratio are well above the sector and what we would expect from even fast-growing stocks.

Assuming it is overvalued, as I have, then what would be a fair price?

To calculate this, I will use the Non-GAAP TTM and FWD ratios, which are well above what we expect (and the GAAP ratios are even higher because the GAAP EPS is lower).

And, I will calculate a low-high range using P/E ratios of 40 and 60, ratios that we might expect from a stock showing strong growth (at least on the top line).

First, we will set an earnings estimate based on the current non-GAAP P/E ratio. At midday on January 22, Axon traded at $606.40 and had a forward P/E of 96.65. That implies EPS of $6.27 ($606.40 / 96.65).

An investor interested in buying at a P/E of 40.00 would be willing to pay up to $250.80 (40 x $6.27).

A more aggressive investor who would buy at a P/E of 60 might pay $376.20 or less (60 x $6.27).

Obviously a price in the $600s or even $500s would be a significant reach, even for more aggressive investors.

Using OmniCalculator, Axon’s intrinsic value is $354.55:

Sources: EPS as calculated above; Q3 revenue growth rate; Moody’s Seasoned Aaa Corporate Bond Yield; and price at midday on January 22, 2026.

Given the relationship between the current price and Axon’s earnings, but keeping in mind its potential to deliver big capital gains again, I rate it a Hold. Five other Seeking Alpha analysts have provided ratings in the past 90 days: one Strong Buy and four Buys.

Wall Street analysts have an average target price of $815.00, which would be an increase of 34.73%. They have 10 Strong Buys, eight Buys, and one Hold. Curiously, they also have two Up revisions and 15 Down revisions.

The Quant system has a Sell rating.

Risk factors

The share price may continue to fall, as it has for five of the past six days. The Q4-2025 and full-year report will come out in about a month and without some good news about net income or EPS, may plunge again.

Most of its products and services compete with those of other companies, and in many areas, the competition is intense. With that competition comes a need to invest in R&D to produce successful new products or to add features to existing product/services.

As noted, its net income and earnings have been affected by tariffs, and any increase in tariffs could lead to lower sales industry-wide. In addition, there is a possibility that tariffs, in combination with one or more other economic factors, might lead to a recession.

Conclusion

I agree with the Quant system warning about Axon Enterprise being overpriced, and as a result of that overpricing, experiencing decelerating momentum. It is a solid company with a history of rewarding shareholders with capital gains, but compensation and tariffs are dragging down its bottom lines.

However, I do not agree with the Quant Sell rating, because I believe it remains a well-managed company, one that still has the potential to reward shareholders. But, until it can turn around its earnings, I will rate it a Hold.

data.title: VCLT: Learn About This Long Dated Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF
data.description:
Introduction

One of the cornerstones of fixed income is represented by corporate investment-grade bonds. While the first known corporate bond was issued in 1623 by the Dutch East India Company, the first recorded investment grade bond came 'only' in 1909 when John Moody started publishing his rating volume.

The Vanguard Long-Term Corporate Bond Index Fund ETF Shares (VCLT) is an exchange-traded fund focused on such credits. The ETF is a passive one, aiming to track the performance of an Index:

VCLT seeks to track the performance of the Bloomberg U.S. 10+ Year Corporate Index. The ETF provides for diversified exposure to the long-term investment-grade U.S. corporate bond market.

The fund comes with a long duration of 12.1 years, and represents a complementary product to the better known iShares iBoxx $ Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD), which has a 8.1 years duration.

In today's article, which is part of the Seeking Alpha educational series, we are going to take a closer look at VCLT, while also doing a deep dive into corporate credit spreads and investment grade bond pricing. These theoretical aspects will help investors better understand the risk drivers behind VCLT and provide a better basis of understanding bond funds.

Credit Spreads Primer

Although it sounds very complex, credit spreads are not rocket science. In this section we are going to try to present both the theoretical explanation for credit spreads as well as the layman one. Let us start with a simple example.

Let us say your neighbor needs some cash to pay for a new garage, and he cannot go to a bank for various reasons. He asks you for $10,000 for a period of one year. There are two questions to ponder here — will he be able to repay the loan, and two what rate should you charge? Let us assume he will be able to repay the loan. The rate you should charge him is the next quandary. In simple terms, you should look at what a bank provides as a rate for certificates of deposit (CDs) and then charge him more. Why? Because the bank CD rate is risk-free (FDIC insured), while your neighbor is not. The difference between what you end up charging your neighbor and the CD rate is called a credit spread.

The theoretical finance definition for a credit spread is the following:

A credit spread in bonds is the yield difference between a corporate bond and a U.S. Treasury bond of similar maturity, reflecting the extra compensation investors demand for taking on the corporate bond's higher default risk.

Going back to our layman example, let us say that you have a second neighbor who also needs a $10,000 loan, but that second neighbor has a lower paying job and known money issues. In this example, without knowing anything about finance, you would charge your second neighbor a higher rate due to perceived risk. In other words, the credit spread for the second neighbor would be higher.

Credit spreads represent the backbone of bonds pricing, and more importantly when VCLT is concerned, investment grade bond pricing:

Most bonds are priced relative to a benchmark. Some of the most common pricing benchmarks are on-the-run U.S. Treasuries (the most current series). Many bonds are priced relative to a specific Treasury bond. For example, the on-the-run 10-year Treasury might be used as the pricing benchmark for a 10-year corporate bond issue.

So the formula for investment grade bond pricing is Benchmark (Treasuries) + Credit Spread. For example, a company placing a 10-year IG bond would price the respective debt at the prevailing 10-year market rate, plus the prevailing credit spread for that rating band. Let us do a theoretical pricing right now:

Above is the current U.S. yield curve, taken from the Market Data section of the Seeking Alpha platform. Let us further assume the company that is issuing the bond is rated BBB:

The above graph shows the current average BBB corporate spreads. Therefore, if our company is an average one, we are going to price their bond at 10Y Treasuries + 0.95%, or better said 4.23% + 0.95% = 5.18%. The 5.18% figure will represent the coupon on the new bond.

Let us look at a real life example to illustrate that:

Antero Resources Corporation (AR) is a natural gas E&P company that just placed on January 14, 2026 a 10-year bond at a 5.4% coupon. The coupon is slightly higher than our calculated yield because Antero is rated BBB- rather than BBB. So that simply means the credit spread for Antero is slightly higher than the market's 0.95% for pure BBB names. When the investment bank that placed the Antero bonds calculated the coupon, they did the very same calculation we outlined above.

Remembering the Inverse Relationship Between Credit Spreads and Bond Prices

One important aspect to always remember in terms of fixed income instruments is the inverse relationship between spreads and bond prices, if Treasury yields are constant. And here we are referencing both credit spreads and all-in yields (i.e., credit spread plus risk-free rate):

As yields move higher, bond prices move lower. The above graph shows a very intuitive visual representation of the respective relationship.

So as a bond investor, you always want to buy bonds when spreads and yields are high, so that when they move lower, your bond portfolio moves up in value.

Current VCLT Composition and Analytics

VCLT is a corporate bond fund with the following current composition:

The ETF is well diversified, with over 2,500 bonds, and has a long duration of 12.1 years. Do note that maturity and duration are not the same, with investors needing to focus on duration because that gives the sensitivity of a fund to interest rates.

VCLT is currently composed of bonds coming from the following sectors:

  • Industrial: 67.8%
  • Finance: 16.8%
  • Utilities: 13.7%
  • Other: 1.3%
  • Treasuries: 0.5%

From a ratings perspective the ETF is investment grade, with a concentration of credits in the A and BBB buckets:

Over 87% of the fund holdings fall in the A/BBB buckets, thus representing the riskiest investment grade bonds.

Other notable metrics are as follows, at the time of writing:

  • AUM: $8.3 billion
  • Expense ratio: 0.03%
  • Duration: 12.1 years
  • Average Maturity: 22 years
  • Portfolio YTM: 5.65%
  • SEC Yield: 5.7%
  • 3Y Standard Deviation: 12.6%

The ETF has a very low expense ratio of only 0.03%, and comes with a high AUM just shy of $9 billion.

Tight Spreads Amid Concerns with the Long End of the Curve

We talked extensively in a different article regarding the three segments of the U.S. yield curve:

  1. Front End (Short End): 1-3 years tenors
  2. The Belly: 3-10 years tenors
  3. The Back End (Long End): 10-30 years tenors

VCLT falls in the long end when it comes to the yield curve, and the easiest way to think about this fund is as a portfolio of bonds with a 22-year maturity which were priced at a spread to equivalent maturity treasuries. Using the 20-year treasury note as a proxy, we see the yield for that duration at 4.8%. The implied credit spread, therefore, for the VCLT portfolio is a mere 0.85%, but please keep in mind that more than half of the fund is AA/A rated.

While the Fed controls the front end of the yield curve, the market controls the long end. Thus, fiscal deficit concerns and inflationary concerns can keep 20-year yields elevated even as the Fed lowers Fed Funds. As a visual representation, please see the tight correlation between VCLT and a long-duration treasuries-only ETF:

The iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) is composed of treasuries only, and has a duration of 15.5 years.

From a macro standpoint, VCLT has more downside than upside currently, given the very tight credit spreads that we are witnessing historically. Even if the Fed cuts rates via Fed Funds, long duration treasury yields can stay elevated because they are not controlled by the Fed.

Risks for the Vanguard Long-Term Corporate Bond Fund

The main risk for VCLT is represented by an inflationary recession environment. A sticky inflation picture means long rates will stay elevated, all while credit spreads will move higher. VCLT would therefore be hit as the required all-in yield would go up. For 100 bps of higher credit spreads, the fund is set to lose -12% in price if risk-free rates stay constant.

The second risk factor is a normal recession. Even in a 'normal' recession, long end treasury rates do not move down as fast as necessary to compensate for higher credit spreads; thus, the ETF would be set to have a lower NAV and price.

Key Advantages and Disadvantages

Advantages:

  • Exposure to the long end of the yield curve and credit spreads
  • Ability to record significant gains if long rates move lower
  • A spread over treasuries
  • Very high diversification from an issuer perspective
  • High daily trading volume and liquidity

Disadvantages:

  • High volatility and standard deviation
  • Potential for price stagnation even if Fed Funds move lower
  • Potential for over -10% drawdowns in a stagflation

Reference Literature

For retail investors eager to learn more about some of the topics discussed above, here are some interesting research materials:

  • Introduction to Financial Analysis - Credit Spreads
  • Understanding Corporate Credit Spreads
  • Morgan Stanly - An introduction to fixed income

Conclusion

VCLT is a passive corporate bond ETF targeting long duration bonds. It currently has a 12.1-year duration and is composed of investment grade bonds. The article discusses credit spreads and investment grade bond pricing as it relates to the VCLT portfolio. The fund comes with a high volatility given its long duration, and could experience a significant drawdown in a stagflationary environment.

This article answers three main questions about VCLT:

  1. What kind of bonds are in VCLT's portfolio?
  2. What influences VCLT's NAV and yield?
  3. What risks and opportunities are associated with holding VCLT?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Tech Rebalance And Volatility Catalysts: Dow Jones And U.S. Stock Index Outlook
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Stocks are back to quite a decent outlook, and markets are all out on yet another phase of the debasement trade, which just took silver to $100 and gold to $5,000.

However, as we head into the weekend, some factors of uncertainty could create significant volatility and change the current market picture in high fashion. One of the first and most evident yet easily overlooked is the federal chair appointment announced by the US President.

As evidenced in this remarkable piece, Trump suggested a decision around his return to the White House from Davos (and he just came back). The race is between Rick Rieder, BlackRock's CEO, and a well-positioned Wall Street veteran who could be the surprise. His appointment would reassure the US dollar and bond markets the most.

On the other hand, Kevin Warsh, the heavy favorite, an ex-Governor at the Fed from 2006-2011 (rough period), could maintain the current trend. Any communications regarding the decision will be market-rocking.

A second risk catalyst for this weekend is the high potential for geopolitical catalysts as we conclude a period full of headlines – heavy US military assets are concentrating in the Middle East, and my thesis is that the Greenland issue was being offered as a distraction.

On that aspect, oil is up 2.50% and still rebounding at its risk premium support area. We will see more on this this weekend – if nothing happens, expect stocks to keep up their path higher.

A third potential volatility contributor could be current market levels – with gold reaching $5,000, silver exploding by 250% to $100, and stock markets at all-time highs, one small profit-taking wave can turn into a significant selling cascade, particularly as we approach the FOMC.

All three factors, when combined, could significantly affect the market picture, so keep an eye on how they interact.

In terms of today's action, US consumer sentiment just beat expectations, and inflation expectations cooled down, usually a positive for the market, but with key tech earnings (helping Nasdaq to outperform) and the Fed meeting approaching (January 28), don't expect a straightforward path ahead.

As a reminder, Microsoft (MSFT), Meta (META), Tesla (TSLA), and ASML (ASML) report after the close on FOMC day, while Apple (AAPL) and Visa (V) follow on Thursday.

Magnificent 7s and semiconductors are doing the heavy lifting in today's sessions. Microsoft is leading its peers to the upside, while defensives and financials drag lower as their earnings season is now complete for the most part.

Dive into our daily session charts and trading levels for the major US indexes: Dow Jones, Nasdaq, and S&P 500 – watch for weekend volatility as the situation is not fully clear yet and Iran remains a factor.

Dow Jones 4H Chart

The Dow just rejected its all-time highs, not so surprising as we head into the weekend, and the direction has mostly stayed rangebound since 2026. The range, extending between 48,000 and 49,700 provides levels of interest for upcoming action:

  • A clear break above should see fast-paced continuation, first to the 50,000 milestone and surely higher (particularly if the recent doubts and uncertainties get resolved).
  • Any weekly close below 48,000 can precede more downside, potentially extending to 45,000.

Nasdaq 4H Chart

Nasdaq is now taking the lead (for the first time in a while) in today's action, as traders reposition ahead of next week's high-expectation earnings.

The pressure is high for the tech-heavy index: These early 2026 reports will reflect on this current year's projections and 2025 numbers as P/E Ratios reach elevated levels, particularly for Mag 7s.

The index is still showing a decent rebound in today's session, and looking at the previous days' action, a breakout of its 24,000 to 25,800 range could be close. A measured move hints at a retest of the all time high records.

This will, of course, need to be accompanied by positive (or at least not too pessimistic) fundamentals – a break and close above the 25,850 level should see continuation.

Next week's earnings will have a key influence on the Nasdaq movement. Keep a close eye on the Fed's communications next week.

data.title: Run It Like It's Hot
data.description:
By Christopher Puplava, CRPC

Stay the Course or Take Profits?

Last year capped a remarkable three-year run for equities, with the S&P 500 rising roughly 86% since the close of the 2022 mini-bear market. After such strong gains, a natural question arises: is it time to take profits and protect accumulated returns, or remain invested for further upside?

To answer this, we must ground our outlook in economic fundamentals and objective market signals. Based on supportive monetary and fiscal policy, both here and abroad, we believe the stage is set for continued economic growth in 2026. This is also a U.S. mid-term election year, and with political control at stake, policy incentives are aligned toward sustaining economic momentum and financial market stability. In short, conditions favor running the economy “hot,” not cooling it.

While our outlook for 2026 remains bullish, as capital allocators, we must remain vigilant to bear market risks. Protecting client capital requires understanding what types of bear markets exist, how they form, and whether current conditions resemble their historical precursors.

Understanding Bear Markets

Historically, bear markets fall into three broad categories:

  1. Cyclical bear markets, driven by economic slowdowns or recessions that reduce consumer spending and corporate earnings.
  2. Structural bear markets, which are longer and deeper, such as the Great Depression of the 1930s, the inflationary bear market of the 1970s, or the tech bubble collapse in the early 2000s.
  3. Event-driven bear markets, which are rarer and typically short-lived, such as the 1998 Asian Currency Crisis, the LTCM collapse, or the 2020 COVID-19 shock.

Event-driven declines are difficult to predict and typically recover quickly. Cyclical and structural bear markets, however, usually exhibit identifiable warning signs — notably monetary tightening, liquidity withdrawal, deteriorating credit conditions, or extreme valuations combined with policy restraint. Aside from high valuations, the other conditions are not present today.

Valuations Are High — But Liquidity Remains Supportive

By traditional valuation measures, the U.S. stock market is expensive. Using the S&P 500 price-to-sales ratio as a simple yardstick:

  • Tech bubble peak (2000): ~2x sales
  • Pre-financial crisis peak (2007): ~1.5x sales
  • Pre-2022 mini-bear market peak: ~3x sales
  • Current level: ~3.4x sales — the highest in more than 35 years

One could reasonably argue that U.S. equities display bubble-like valuation characteristics. However, valuation alone does not end bull markets. Historically, major peaks occur when elevated valuations collide with monetary tightening — that is, when the Federal Reserve “takes away the punch bowl.”

That is not today’s environment. The Fed is cutting interest rates and expanding its balance sheet by approximately $40 billion per month — a stark contrast to the tightening that preceded both the 2000 and 2007 peaks and contributed to the 2022 mini bear market. In our view, this makes the probability of a near-term structural bear market low.

Most cyclical bear markets are also preceded by monetary tightening. Instead, current conditions show multiple forms of ongoing stimulus:

  • Central bank easing via lower short-term rates
  • Commercial bank lending at a three-year high
  • Mortgage rates declining from ~8% in 2023 to just above 6% today
  • Falling oil and gasoline prices
  • Fiscal stimulus via an estimated $200B in consumer tax rebates

These forces point toward economic acceleration, not contraction.

Market Internals: The Leaves on the Tree

The stock market provides continuous feedback for those willing to listen. One useful analogy is a tree through changing seasons. When autumn approaches, leaves change color and begin to fall — long before the tree appears bare. Similarly, bear markets often begin internally: individual stocks weaken while headline indices still rise. Eventually, more stocks fall than rise, and the broader market follows.

Currently, we see the opposite. Participation across the market is stronger than at any point last year. A large percentage of stocks in the Russell 3000 remain above their 200-day moving averages — a key measure of long-term trend health. Credit markets, which typically deteriorate before stock markets fall, show no stress; credit spreads remain contained.

Broad participation and healthy credit conditions are hallmarks of economic acceleration. These signals validate, rather than contradict, our bullish outlook.

Positioning: “Skate to where the puck is going to be, not where it has been” – Wayne Gretzky

Our bullish thesis is currently demonstrated by our stock exposure near maximum thresholds, with an underweight toward bonds. The risk of an overheating economy is renewed inflation, which we hedge against through our commodity exposure, as well as a reduced exposure toward bonds, as rising inflation is typically coupled with rising bond yields, which hurt bond prices.

Beyond asset class exposure, we have deliberately taken profits in positions that have significantly appreciated and are no longer undervalued, allowing us to seek out new opportunities with greater value potential. As mentioned previously, just as there are tells for the changing seasons, we see tells for the changing business cycle. One area that is often the first to detect a shift in monetary policy is precious metals, which had a banner year last year and correctly foresaw the Fed shifting toward an easing policy but also likely benefited from geopolitical uncertainty.

Monetary and fiscal stimulus influence the economy and markets with a lag. In 2019, gold took off before the Fed began to expand its balance sheet and was quick to respond to the Fed’s intervention during the COVID pandemic of 2020, rallying to an all-time high of over $2,000 an ounce. However, just as the economy was starting to come back in mid-2020, gold peaked and sold off 20% into 2021. There was a clear handoff from precious metals to other commodities like energy and base metals as the economy came roaring back, where the CRB Raw Industrials Index (composed of base metals, textiles, and raw materials) rallied over 70% in the following two years (lower panel below).

Just as gold and precious metals outperformed other commodities during the 2019–2020 cycle, they have once again taken the lead. After a period of stagnation since 2020, gold broke out to an all-time high in 2024 (top panel below) and has delivered a remarkable performance since then. In our view, gold appears to be leading other commodities by roughly two years, with the broader CRB Raw Industrials Index recently breaking out of a three-year base (bottom panel) and now potentially poised to retest its 2022 highs.

Precious metals—particularly gold—have historically acted as early indicators of broader capital shifts. When we push gold forward by two years, we can see how the CRB Raw Industrials Index appears to be following gold’s path, which is one of the reasons our firm has diversified our commodity exposure.

Most notably, this two-year lead between gold and the broader commodity complex is also seen with stocks. Looking at the GDX Gold Miners ETF (red line below, advanced two years), we can see a strong two-year leading correlation with energy stocks using the XOP Oil & Gas ETF (black line below). We believe the puck will be skating toward other commodities beyond precious metals, which is why we have shifted our commodity exposure. Depending on the client's objective, we have exposure to copper, aluminum, iron ore, agriculture and energy.

Beyond commodities, in the last few months, we have also reallocated away from the technology sector and toward more value-oriented sectors that follow the business cycle, such as industrials and consumer stocks. Growth stocks like technology companies tend to outperform value stocks when overall economic growth is anemic or declining and command a premium for their persistent growth. However, value stocks tend to have volatile earnings that ebb and flow with the economy, and so when growth is not scarce and the economy accelerates, they often outperform growth stocks. This is why we have shifted to value stocks, considering our bullish economic outlook.

While a strengthening economy is a key catalyst for potential outperformance of value stocks over growth stocks this year, we may be approaching a significant regime shift—one in which value stocks could outperform growth not only in the near term but potentially for years to come. If our assessment of this regime shift proves accurate, it will be imperative for portfolio managers—many of whom have maintained significant overweights in technology and growth sectors over the past decade—to recognize and adapt to this evolving market environment.

Is the US Dollar in Another Secular Bear Market?

While value stocks typically outperform growth stocks in an accelerating economy, they also tend to excel during periods of U.S. Dollar (USD) weakness. This is primarily because commodity-related sectors—such as energy and materials—are classified as value stocks and are more heavily represented in value indexes than in growth indexes. These sectors stand to benefit from a weaker USD.

Additionally, value sectors generally have a higher proportion of export-oriented businesses compared to growth sectors. As the USD declines, revenues generated abroad—when converted back into dollars—increase, providing a tailwind for these sectors. This dynamic is a key reason why value stocks tend to benefit in a weak USD environment.

This relationship is evident when comparing the USD Index (DXY, black line below) with the relative performance of the S&P 500 Growth Index (SGX) versus the S&P 500 Value Index (SVX) (red line below). In the 1990s, as the USD appreciated, growth stocks outperformed value. Conversely, during the 2000s, a declining USD boosted commodity prices and disproportionately benefited value stocks. However, following the stabilization of the USD Index in 2008—marking the end of a prolonged secular bear market—growth stocks regained leadership and outperformed value for nearly two decades as the USD entered a secular bull market.

Just as getting the economy correct is important for asset allocation and portfolio construction, so too is getting the direction of the USD. We have seen three major secular bear markets and three major secular bull markets in the USD Index over the last half century, as shown below. We are closely monitoring the USD Index in the months ahead to quickly identify any major secular shift—either confirming that our current portfolio allocations remain appropriate or signaling the need to adjust, such as reducing commodity exposure and reallocating toward growth sectors.

When we zoom in on the USD and its recent secular bull market, we can see that it began in 2011 and has been in a well-defined rising trend channel. When it hits the bottom of the channel, it rises, and when it hits the top end of the channel, it falls. The USD Index had a major breakout in 2022 by surging to multi-decade highs when it exceeded the $100 price level (horizontal red line below). That key level remained as support for the next three years, as it rallied every time it tested $100. However, in mid-2025, that level was broken and has remained key resistance to every USD rally since then. The USD Index is currently being squeezed between this key $100 resistance level and the rising secular lower trend channel. A break below this level would confirm our suspicion that the secular bull market in the USD that began in 2011 is over, where 2022 marked the top, and that we are now in a secular bear market for the USD, likely for years to come.

The Demographic Dollar Decline

While this is our thesis, we must have corroboration and will adapt client portfolios should our belief prove premature. The catalyst that could end the secular bull market for the USD and help explain gold’s performance is a major expansion in the Federal Reserve’s balance sheet and the supply of USDs. The Federal Reserve, in its own research papers, predicted that it would have to expand its balance sheet and keep its size roughly 20% of the economy as measured by Gross Domestic Product (GDP). However, if foreign demand for U.S. Treasuries (USTs) and the USD continues to decline, the Fed may need to increase asset purchases even further, effectively expanding the money supply at an accelerated pace.

Foreign official holdings of U.S. Treasuries have declined from over $3 trillion in 2021 to $2.7 trillion today. While this reduction may appear modest, it is significant in the context of the U.S. public debt expanding from $28 trillion to $38 trillion over the same period. In other words, as the U.S. increased its outstanding debt by $10 trillion in the past five years, foreign official institutions not only refrained from absorbing any of this new issuance but were actually net sellers of Treasuries.

Given current demographic trends, entitlement spending for Medicare and Social Security is projected to accelerate, driving even larger federal deficits in the years ahead. If foreign demand for U.S. debt continues to decline, financing these deficits will become increasingly challenging. Since 2020, China has reduced its U.S. Treasury holdings by over $400 billion, leaving Japan as the largest foreign creditor with approximately $1.2 trillion in holdings. However, Japan’s holdings have remained flat over the past five years, and with its own rising debt burden and increasing domestic bond yields, there is a real risk that Japanese investors may repatriate capital to support their local bond market. Together, these twin demographic and fiscal pressures in both the U.S. and Japan could be the primary catalysts propelling the U.S. dollar into a prolonged secular decline through the end of the decade.

Risks and Opportunities

Given the level of U.S. indebtedness and the ongoing increase in debt issuance, a key risk we continue to monitor is the potential for a significant breakout in bond yields. Over the past three years, the 30-year U.S. Treasury yield has remained range-bound between approximately 4% and 5%. A breakout to new highs in the 30-year yield would likely exert downward pressure on the equity market, especially if accompanied by a strengthening U.S. dollar. Notably, the combination of rising interest rates (red line below) and a surging USD (green line below) contributed to the 2022 equity bear market (black line, red-shaded region). Conversely, as interest rates and the USD Index peaked in October 2022, equity markets stabilized and the bear market came to an end (yellow shaded region).

These two areas, bond yields and the USD, will be our key tells for whether to maintain an aggressive stance or shift course and raise cash as we navigate 2026.

As is widely recognized, Fed Chairman Jerome Powell’s term concludes in May, providing President Trump with the opportunity to appoint a successor. Given that this is a mid-term election year, it is highly probable that Trump will select a candidate who shares his preference for lower interest rates to stimulate economic growth. Chairman Powell initiated a rate-cutting cycle in 2024, which could readily continue under a more dovish Fed Chair. Even in the absence of additional cuts, the lagged effects of the 2024–2025 easing cycle may still influence financial markets this year, potentially serving as a bullish catalyst.

Historically, changes in the Federal Reserve’s policy rate have influenced investor behavior with a two-year lag, drawing capital out of financial markets and into money market funds during tightening cycles and encouraging movement out of cash and back into risk assets during easing cycles. This relationship is illustrated below with the Federal Funds Rate (black line, advanced two years) and money market assets (blue line). Currently, nearly $8 trillion is parked in money market funds, and 2026 falls within the historical window when investors and savers may begin reallocating from cash to financial markets. With President Trump signaling a desire to “run the economy hot,” this potential rotation of capital could provide a further boost to equities, especially in the context of a strong economic backdrop.

Summary

In summary, the investment landscape entering 2026 remains favorable for continued economic expansion and equity market strength. Supportive monetary and fiscal policy, improving credit conditions, broadening market participation, and accelerating economic momentum all reinforce our constructive outlook. While valuations are elevated and risks always exist, the historical precursors to major cyclical or structural bear markets are not presently in place.

Accordingly, we remain positioned to participate in further upside while maintaining disciplined risk management and closely monitoring key market “tells” that would signal the need to adjust course. Our focus remains steadfast: protecting capital during periods of stress while capturing potential opportunities during periods of expansion.
